items.correct: The first five books of the Hebrew Bible (Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy) — traditionally attributed to Moses and containing law, narrative and covenant.
items.misconception: The Torah is the same as the Book of Genesis.
items.random_wrong: The Torah is a single scroll containing every Jewish text.
items.why: The Torah is five books, not one. Wider Jewish scripture (Tanakh) also includes Nevi'im (Prophets) and Ketuvim (Writings). Students often equate Torah with Genesis or with the whole Hebrew Bible.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What does the teleological (design) argument claim?
items.correct: Apparent purpose, order or complexity in the universe implies an intelligent designer — Paley's watchmaker analogy is the classic example.
items.misconception: The design argument proves God created the world in seven days.
items.random_wrong: The design argument proves evolution is false.
items.why: The argument is philosophical, not anti-evolutionary. Many religious thinkers accept evolution as the designer's mechanism — students conflate design with young-earth creationism.
The answer examiners want is: Both. “Augustine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Struggle. “War” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what century did Crusades begin?
items.why: The real figure is 11 century. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
